did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning algorithms, specifically a custom CNN and four pre-trained transfer learning architectures (VGG16, ResNet50, MobileNetV2, and InceptionV3), to perform melanoma detection and classification on dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the design, training, and comparative evaluation of five deep learning models (custom CNN, VGG16, ResNet50, MobileNetV2, InceptionV3) on the ISIC dataset for melanoma detection. It includes specific methodology, results with performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score), and a conclusion based on these new findings, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep Learning based Framework for Explainable Melanoma Detection in Clinical Applications
journal: Proceedings of the 4th International Conference on Applied Artificial Intelligence and Computing (ICAAIC-2025)
brief_summary: This study developed an automated deep learning framework for explainable melanoma detection using dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset. The researchers compared the performance of a custom CNN against four pre-trained transfer learning architectures: VGG16, ResNet50, MobileNetV2, and InceptionV3. Models were trained with optimized preprocessing, data augmentation, and strategic fine-tuning, and evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. VGG16 and ResNet50 emerged as the most effective models, achieving classification accuracies of 92% and 89.5%, respectively. The framework demonstrates significant potential for clinical applications by providing reliable and transparent diagnostic assistance for early melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on automated melanoma detection using dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset. The classification task is binary, distinguishing between malignant (melanoma) and benign lesions. The dataset structure included 'benign and malignant sub directories'.
Used as the negative class in the binary classification task (benign vs. malignant). The ISIC dataset provided images arranged in 'benign and malignant sub directories'.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into 'benign' or 'malignant' (melanoma) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is a 'binary classification problem (benign vs. malignant)' using the ISIC dataset. Models (Custom CNN, VGG16, ResNet50, MobileNetV2, InceptionV3) were evaluated on accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score for this diagnostic classification task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions, organized into benign and malignant sub-directories.

use.training: Used for training the custom CNN and four pre-trained transfer learning architectures (VGG16, ResNet50, MobileNetV2, InceptionV3) with optimized preprocessing, data augmentation, and strategic fine-tuning.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process to monitor model performance and generalization.
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluation of the models using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate model performance by analyzing true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives for each class.
To ensure a balanced comparison of performance metrics across both classes (benign and malignant) in the comparative analysis.
To calculate overall performance metrics weighted by the number of samples per class.
To counteract overfitting during the fine-tuning phase of model training.
To adjust the learning rate during fine-tuning to improve model convergence and prevent overfitting.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Dataset.notes: ResNet50 secured the highest overall performance with 92% accuracy and 0.92 macro F1-score. VGG16 yielded robust results with 90% accuracy. The custom CNN and MobileNetV2 performed at comparable levels. InceptionV3 produced the weakest performance. ResNet50 showed strong precision (0.94) for malignant class and high recall (0.95) for benign class. InceptionV3 had the highest number of false negatives. MobileNetV2 and InceptionV3 showed signs of overfitting.

architecture_used.main_model: Five models compared: Custom CNN, VGG16, ResNet50, MobileNetV2, and InceptionV3.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models (VGG16, ResNet50, MobileNetV2, InceptionV3) leveraged ImageNet weights. Custom CNN was trained from scratch.
architecture_used.input_size: Images were resized to 299x299 (depending on model architecture) and batched in 32.

architecture_details.Custom CNN: Simple neural network with three processing layers (convolutional blocks), incorporating batch normalization and dropout.
architecture_details.VGG16: Deep pre-trained model with a simple layered structure, modified with custom dense heads.
architecture_details.ResNet50: Pre-trained model with shortcut connections, fine-tuned to specific image features.
architecture_details.MobileNetV2: Lightweight pre-trained model, fast and efficient.
architecture_details.InceptionV3: Complex pre-trained model considering details widely and deeply, fine-tuned.

training_details.learning_rate: 3e-1 for the first phase (classifier head only training); 1e-5 for the second phase (fine-tuning).
training_details.strategy: Two-phase training approach. Phase 1: Classifier head trained, base frozen. Phase 2: Initial convolutional layers unfrozen for fine-tuning with early stopping and learning rate schedule.
training_details.regularization: Early stopping and learning rate schedule to counteract overfitting.

architecture_used.classification_or_output_head: Custom dense heads appended to pre-trained models; binary classification (benign vs. malignant).

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 299x299 pixels (depending on model architecture)
Batching images with a batch size of 32
Normalization/Standardization of pixel values using ImageNet statistics (empirically in range [0,1] or based on specific model needs)
Data augmentation (mentioned as part of optimized preprocessing, but specific techniques not detailed)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Mentioned as part of 'optimized preprocessing' and 'strategic fine-tuning' during training, but specific techniques (e.g., geometric transforms, color shifts, probabilities) were not detailed in the paper.

conclusions: ResNet50 and VGG16 emerged as the most effective architectures for melanoma detection, achieving classification accuracies of 92% and 90% respectively.

ResNet50 demonstrated superior diagnostic performance with a precision of 0.94 for the malignant class and a recall of 0.95 for the benign class, highlighting its suitability for clinical settings where minimizing missed diagnoses is critical.
The custom CNN and MobileNetV2 performed at comparable levels with 88% and 87% accuracy, respectively.
InceptionV3 produced the weakest performance among the tested models, with 82% accuracy and exhibiting signs of overfitting and the highest number of false negatives.
MobileNetV2 showed signs of overfitting, indicated by a degradation in validation accuracy while training accuracy continued to improve.
The study validates the use of deep transfer learning combined with a two-stage training process (fine-tuning) as an effective method for automated melanoma detection.
The framework provides a viable, reliable, and transparent tool for early melanoma diagnosis, addressing the limitations of subjective visual examination by dermatologists.

Future work aims to improve diagnostic accuracy by building an ensemble or weighted average of the most effective models (e.g., ResNet50, VGG16) and refining explainability through advanced visualization methods.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single, small dataset (ISIC) with only 1,000 images (500 benign, 500 malignant), which raises concerns about generalizability and potential dataset bias.
No external validation was performed; the models were evaluated solely on the internal test split of the ISIC dataset.
The paper did not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) to validate the performance differences between models.
Specific data augmentation techniques were not detailed, only described as 'optimized preprocessing' and 'data augmentation'.
The Custom CNN showed training instability and volatility, potentially due to an excessive training rate, limiting its reliability as a baseline.
MobileNetV2 and InceptionV3 exhibited signs of overfitting, indicated by diverging training and validation accuracy/loss curves.
InceptionV3 had the highest number of false negatives, which is a critical clinical limitation for melanoma detection where missing malignant cases is dangerous.
The paper claims 'explainable' detection but only uses Grad-CAM for visualization without rigorous quantitative explainability metrics or clinical user studies.
Deployment concerns are noted as future work; the current framework is not validated for real-time or mobile deployment despite mentioning MobileNetV2's efficiency.
The study focuses only on binary classification (benign vs. malignant) and does not address multi-class classification or other skin lesion types.
